narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 51.8% chance that you would not pass—slightly above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, and background details like your lower-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, without considering gender or racial background. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the 6th decile average among students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential struggle in later undergraduate performance. Next in importance, your LSAT score of 36 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it's close to the 37.3 average for successful bar takers, showing solid potential there. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 helped your case, as it's stronger than the 3.24 average for those who passed, suggesting good foundational academic strength. While your full-time enrollment aligns with most admitted students and your income bracket is represented among passers (though less common than middle or upper-middle), these didn't outweigh the Year 3 decile concern. Compared to typical accepted students, who often have higher mid-to-late undergraduate deciles alongside balanced test scores, your profile appeared riskier for bar success in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your overall potential—just a data-driven flag on one key area. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or post-bac programs that demonstrate improvement. You might also seek bar prep resources early or connect with mentors from similar backgrounds who've succeeded. Reapplying after addressing this could shift your prediction favorably, as the model responds well to upward trends in performance metrics. Many students in your position have turned things around with targeted effort—your above-average undergrad GPA is a great foundation to build on.
